evidence_quotes.quote: One of the most harmful examples is the shutdown of video game servers.
Increasingly, even single-player games are tied to an always-online requirement.
When the publisher decides to discontinue support, the game simply ceases to function.
If online servers are closed, there should be fallback options such as offline play, local hosting, or patches that unlock community solutions.
End User License Agreements (EULAs), which routinely claim the right to revoke access or terminate products unilaterally.
Clarify consumer rights so that companies cannot hide behind one-sided EULAs to strip away ownership.

paragraphs.text: As a consumer and player, I feel that the current digital marketplace leaves us hostages to the decisions of large corporations. We pay full price for products that can later be taken away from us without warning, and this practice is fundamentally unfair. One of the most harmful examples is the shutdown of video game servers. Increasingly, even single-player games are tied to an always-online requirement. When the publisher decides to discontinue support, the game simply ceases to function. What was once a purchased product is suddenly worthless. This is not a matter of lifecycle support consumers do not expect companies to maintain online services forever. What we do expect, however, is that a product we bought remains reasonably usable after official support ends. If online servers are closed, there should be fallback options such as offline play, local hosting, or patches that unlock community solutions. Without such measures, publishers are effectively destroying products that people have paid for, often to push them toward newer titles. In the physical world, such behavior would be unthinkable. Imagine buying a book, only to have the publisher demand that you burn it because they no longer support it. Digital products should not be treated differently simply because they exist in software form. This imbalance of power is worsened by End User License Agreements (EULAs), which routinely claim the right to revoke access or terminate products unilaterally. No reasonable consumer would expect that buying a game also means granting the seller the right to destroy it later. Yet this is the reality we face today. The Digital Fairness Act is an opportunity to restore balance and protect consumers. Specifically, I urge the Commission to: Ban practices that render purchased digital products completely unusable after server shutdowns. Require fallback solutions that leave products in a functional state, even if reduced (e.g., offline play instead of online matchmaking). Clarify consumer rights so that companies cannot hide behind one-sided EULAs to strip away ownership. We deserve digital products that respect the same basic principles of fairness and usability that we take for granted in the physical world.
